title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an engineer called Rebecca Gale talking about her work on an offshore wind farm. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an engineer called Rebecca Gale talking about her work on an offshore wind farm. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Rebecca Gale discusses the engineering marvels and logistical difficulties of maintaining wind turbines at sea.
extracts.audio_script: Hi everyone. My name is Rebecca Gale and I’m a structural engineer. I spend most of my working life thirty miles out at sea, on one of the world's largest offshore wind farms. [7]I first decided to pursue a career in renewable energy after reading an article about the engineering of wind turbines[/7]. It seemed like the perfect way to combine my love for physics with environmentalism. Maintaining these giants is no small task. Each turbine is over 200 metres tall. [8]I found that the most intimidating part of the job was the initial climb up the internal ladder[/8]. It’s a long way up, and you can feel the whole structure swaying in the wind. Safety is our top priority. We use advanced sensors to monitor the health of the blades. [9]The discovery of a small hairline crack in a blade was a moment of high pressure for the team[/9]. We had to organize an emergency repair in the middle of a storm. Logistically, everything is difficult at sea. [10]We rely on a specialized heavy-lift vessel to transport the massive components to the site[/10]. These ships are engineering wonders in themselves. Life on the service platform is surprisingly comfortable. [11]I was pleased to find that the excellent gym facilities helped us stay fit during long shifts[/11]. You need to be in good physical shape for this work. One of the most beautiful aspects of the job is the wildlife. [12]Observing a pod of dolphins playing near the turbine foundations was a truly magical experience[/12]. It reminds you why we are doing this. Looking to the future, the technology is evolving. [13]I believe that floating wind farms are the key to unlocking the power of the deep ocean[/13]. They don't need to be fixed to the seabed, which opens up vast new areas. As we often say at the office, [14]we are not just generating power, we are 'harvesting the gale' for a cleaner tomorrow[/14]. It’s a challenging but incredibly rewarding career.

dialogue.speaker: rebecca
dialogue.text: Hi everyone. My name is Rebecca Gale and I’m a structural engineer. I spend most of my working life thirty miles out at sea, on one of the world's largest offshore wind farms. I first decided to pursue a career in renewable energy after reading an article about the engineering of wind turbines. It seemed like the perfect way to combine my love for physics with environmentalism. Maintaining these giants is no small task. Each turbine is over 200 metres tall. I found that the most intimidating part of the job was the initial climb up the internal ladder. It’s a long way up, and you can feel the whole structure swaying in the wind. Safety is our top priority. We use advanced sensors to monitor the health of the blades. The discovery of a small hairline crack in a blade was a moment of high pressure for the team. We had to organize an emergency repair in the middle of a storm. Logistically, everything is difficult at sea. We rely on a specialized heavy-lift vessel to transport the massive components to the site. These ships are engineering wonders in themselves. Life on the service platform is surprisingly comfortable. I was pleased to find that the excellent gym facilities helped us stay fit during long shifts. You need to be in good physical shape for this work. One of the most beautiful aspects of the job is the wildlife. Observing a pod of dolphins playing near the turbine foundations was a truly magical experience. It reminds you why we are doing this. Looking to the future, the technology is evolving. I believe that floating wind farms are the key to unlocking the power of the deep ocean. They don't need to be fixed to the seabed, which opens up vast new areas. As we often say at the office, we are not just generating power, we are 'harvesting the gale' for a cleaner tomorrow. It’s a challenging but incredibly rewarding career.

questions.question: Rebecca’s interest in wind energy was triggered by an (7) she read.
questions.explanation: She mentions reading an article about the engineering of wind turbines.

questions.question: Rebecca admits that she found the (8) to be the most frightening part of her daily routine.
questions.explanation: She says the initial climb up the internal ladder was the most intimidating part.

questions.question: The team had to work quickly after they found a (9) on one of the turbines.
questions.explanation: The discovery of a small hairline crack in a blade was a high-pressure moment.

questions.question: A (10) is necessary to move the large parts of the wind turbine across the ocean.
questions.explanation: They rely on a specialized heavy-lift vessel for transportation.

questions.question: Rebecca was happy that the (11) provided on the platform allowed the crew to exercise.
questions.explanation: She mentions that excellent gym facilities helped them stay fit.

questions.question: Rebecca describes an encounter with (12) as one of the highlights of her time at sea.
questions.explanation: Observing a pod of dolphins was a magical experience for her.

questions.question: According to Rebecca, (13) will allow wind energy to expand into deeper waters.
questions.explanation: She believes floating wind farms are the key to unlocking the deep ocean.

questions.question: Rebecca uses the expression (14) to describe the work her company does.
questions.explanation: She says they are 'harvesting the gale' for a cleaner tomorrow.
